items.title: GFHK now expects the AMD–Anthropic deal to be larger than the OAI and Meta deals. 👀👀
items.content:
GFHK now expects the AMD–Anthropic deal to be larger than the OAI and Meta deals. 👀👀Jeff Pu (@sssjeffpu)Abstract of AMD note

TP increased to $640 (based on 45x 2027E EPS or 32x 2028E EPS), with EPS revised up by +2%/+11% for 2026E/2027E.

Advancing AI & Customers: AMD is expected to unveil Anthropic as its third GW-level customer, likely exceeding Meta/OpenAI deal scales in 2027, alongside Microsoft's deployment of the Helios rack-scale solution announced on July 20. As such, we believe 2027 its GPU demand visibility is 40–50% above current CoWoS supply, suggesting room for upward revision.

GPU Roadmap: AMD is narrowing the hardware gap with the MI450X and pulling ahead of Nvidia with the MI550X (featuring a 4-die package and larger HBM).

Datacenter CPU & Venice: Venice's strong CPU cycle, driven by a robust supply chain build plan of ~5m in 2027 and higher server CPU TAM, is expected to lift datacenter CPU ASPs by 30–35% YoY in 2027. Near term likely a price hike in late-3Q26.

Upcoming Earnings: The earnings call on Aug 4 is anticipated to be a non-event with 2Q/3Q revenue expected at $11.4bn/$12.2bn and 3Q margin moving higher. 

 #AMD   #AI —  https://x.com/sssjeffpu/status/2079559887496036854 
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: Quoted from a JPM seminar (a must-read): - Rubin Ultra, which is based on a 5.5x reticle size, has encountered difficulties in implementation testing, and the expert believes that a 9.5x implementation will be extremely difficult. As of the first half of 2027, soldering has been identified as an issue for the 9.5x reticle. Evaluations of Rubin Ultra with a 5.5x reticle are currently underway, but the expert said they are not progressing well. The expert also explained that if CoWoS-L with a 9.5x reticle is unsuccessful, limitations in packaging technology could make it difficult to realize Nvidia’s Feynman generation in 2029. - EMIB-T is a structure in which through-silicon vias (TSVs) are f
items.content:
Quoted from a JPM seminar (a must-read):

- Rubin Ultra, which is based on a 5.5x reticle size, has encountered difficulties in implementation testing, and the expert believes that a 9.5x implementation will be extremely difficult. As of the first half of 2027, soldering has been identified as an issue for the 9.5x reticle. Evaluations of Rubin Ultra with a 5.5x reticle are currently underway, but the expert said they are not progressing well. The expert also explained that if CoWoS-L with a 9.5x reticle is unsuccessful, limitations in packaging technology could make it difficult to realize Nvidia’s Feynman generation in 2029.

- EMIB-T is a structure in which through-silicon vias (TSVs) are formed in a silicon bridge, enabling signals and power to be delivered from the substrate side to HBM and LSI. The ability to optimize power delivery on the bridge helps suppress power noise in HBM operating at high frequencies and low voltages. In addition, HBM and LSI components with different sizes and connection requirements can be mounted individually on the substrate without using a large interposer. This eliminates the need to bond an entire large module at once, as required with CoWoS-L, making the overall interposer less susceptible to warpage and offering advantages in back-end process yields. According to the expert, Intel claims that EMIB-T can support reticle sizes of up to 12x and could serve as an alternative to CoWoS-L.

 $INTC   $TSM 
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: Interesting. Hybrid bonding is expected to be used for Feynman, which will feature custom HBM (cHBM). It appears the technology has been pushed back from conventional HBM4E.
items.content:
Interesting. Hybrid bonding is expected to be used for Feynman, which will feature custom HBM (cHBM). It appears the technology has been pushed back from conventional HBM4E.Jukan (@jukan05)Samsung Electronics Begins Construction of Hybrid Bonding Mass-Production Line at Pyeongtaek P5

Samsung Electronics has begun constructing a die-to-wafer (D2W) hybrid bonding mass-production line at its Pyeongtaek Campus. The line will be used to manufacture next-generation high-bandwidth memory (HBM) and logic semiconductors.

According to semiconductor industry sources on the 21st, Samsung Electronics has selected Dutch semiconductor back-end equipment maker BE Semiconductor Industries (Besi) as its preferred supplier and initiated the process of acquiring 50 D2W hybrid bonding machines. The equipment is slated for installation at the P5 fab on its Pyeongtaek Campus.

During Besi’s first-quarter earnings conference call in April, CEO Richard Blickman said, “We expect to see something more definitive regarding Samsung Electronics’ adoption of hybrid bonders around the middle of this year.” At the time, Besi’s hybrid bonders were undergoing Samsung’s qualification testing.

However, it will take more time to finalize the order. Samsung has requested modifications to the equipment architecture, making it difficult to determine a specific delivery date. Such modifications involve changing certain specifications—such as the design, components, or modules—to meet a customer’s requirements. The fact that Besi’s hybrid bonders cost approximately KRW 6 billion (about $4.3 million) each, roughly twice the price of competing products, is also said to be prolonging negotiations.

“Besi supplies hybrid bonders to semiconductor companies worldwide, including TSMC and Micron, and is reluctant to modify the equipment architecture exclusively for Samsung,” an industry source said. “Samsung considers Besi’s equipment the best option, but because coordination has been difficult, it is also considering products from Korean equipment makers that can respond more quickly and flexibly.”

SEMES, Samsung Electronics’ semiconductor equipment subsidiary, has also received a request to supply D2W hybrid bonders for the mass-production line. The company hopes to secure orders for two hybrid bonding production lines at the Pyeongtaek Campus.

“SEMES felt a strong sense of crisis when Samsung initially selected Besi’s equipment, but the situation has recently begun to change,” another industry source said.

SEMES completed development of its D2W hybrid bonder early this year and supplied a unit to Samsung Electronics for performance qualification. This suggests that the equipment has passed the testing process.

Samsung is also considering Hanwha Semitech as a hybrid bonder supplier. In February, Hanwha Semitech completed development of its second-generation D2W hybrid bonder, the “SHB2 Nano.” It subsequently supplied an evaluation unit to SK hynix in April.

The equipment was delivered as an integrated cluster system combining the bonder with a wafer-handling equipment front-end module (EFEM) from Cymechs, a plasma activation module from Hanwha Semitech, and a cleaning module from ZEUS.

Samsung’s construction of a D2W hybrid bonding mass-production line at its Pyeongtaek Campus is related to the packaging processes for HBM and logic semiconductors. According to industry sources, Samsung has pursued a hybrid bonding line for next-generation HBM at its Pyeongtaek Campus in addition to its Cheonan Campus, which serves as a major semiconductor back-end packaging hub.

Using hybrid copper bonding (HCB), rather than conventional thermocompression (TC) bonding, to stack HBM core dies (DRAM chips) can significantly improve performance, power consumption, and thermal characteristics. This is because the technology directly bonds copper and dielectric materials without using metal protrusions known as micro-bumps, thereby shortening the interconnect length between devices.

The technology is particularly likely to be used for customer-specific, or custom, HBM (cHBM). In cHBM, Samsung replaces the conventional base die with a logic die incorporating the customer’s computing circuitry and intellectual property (IP). This logic die assists external central processing units (CPUs) and graphics processing units (GPUs) with computation while optimizing data transmission.

Samsung plans to implement a three-dimensional system-in-package (3D SiP) architecture in which HBM DRAM layers are stacked vertically and directly on top of the logic die.

Samsung Electronics’ foundry business also unveiled “3D Cube-H,” a next-generation 3D vertical stacking solution based on hybrid bonding, last month. Designed for next-generation artificial intelligence chips and high-performance computing (HPC) systems, the heterogeneous integration packaging technology offers improved performance, power efficiency, and bandwidth compared with conventional packaging technologies. Samsung Foundry is currently actively proposing the solution to customers for adoption.

Hybrid bonders from the equipment suppliers are expected to begin arriving and being installed at the Pyeongtaek Campus toward the end of this year. However, Samsung internally expects large-scale production using hybrid bonding to become feasible around 2030, as the technology is considerably more challenging than TC bonding.

“We expect hybrid bonding to enter full-scale use around 2029, when Nvidia’s next-generation AI accelerator, Feynman, is expected to arrive,” a third industry source said.—  https://x.com/jukan05/status/2079495315292557629 
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 21, 2026

The equipment is slated for installation at the P5 fab on its Pyeongtaek Campus.

During Besi’s first-quarter earnings conference call in April, CEO Richard Blickman said, “We expect to see something more definitive regarding Samsung Electronics’ adoption of hybrid bonders around the middle of this year.” At the time, Besi’s hybrid bonders were undergoing Samsung’s qualification testing.

However, it will take more time to finalize the order. Samsung has requested modifications to the equipment architecture, making it difficult to determine a specific delivery date. Such modifications involve changing certain specifications—such as the design, components, or modules—to meet a customer’s requirements. The fact that Besi’s hybrid bonders cost approximately KRW 6 billion (about $4.3 million) each, roughly twice the price of competing products, is also said to be prolonging negotiations.

“SEMES felt a strong sense of crisis when Samsung initially selected Besi’s equipment, but the situation has recently begun to change,” another industry source said.

SEMES completed development of its D2W hybrid bonder early this year and supplied a unit to Samsung Electronics for performance qualification. This suggests that the equipment has passed the testing process.

Samsung is also considering Hanwha Semitech as a hybrid bonder supplier. In February, Hanwha Semitech completed development of its second-generation D2W hybrid bonder, the “SHB2 Nano.” It subsequently supplied an evaluation unit to SK hynix in April.

The equipment was delivered as an integrated cluster system combining the bonder with a wafer-handling equipment front-end module (EFEM) from Cymechs, a plasma activation module from Hanwha Semitech, and a cleaning module from ZEUS.

Samsung’s construction of a D2W hybrid bonding mass-production line at its Pyeongtaek Campus is related to the packaging processes for HBM and logic semiconductors. According to industry sources, Samsung has pursued a hybrid bonding line for next-generation HBM at its Pyeongtaek Campus in addition to its Cheonan Campus, which serves as a major semiconductor back-end packaging hub.

The technology is particularly likely to be used for customer-specific, or custom, HBM (cHBM). In cHBM, Samsung replaces the conventional base die with a logic die incorporating the customer’s computing circuitry and intellectual property (IP). This logic die assists external central processing units (CPUs) and graphics processing units (GPUs) with computation while optimizing data transmission.

However, Samsung internally expects large-scale production using hybrid bonding to become feasible around 2030, as the technology is considerably more challenging than TC bonding.

“We expect hybrid bonding to enter full-scale use around 2029, when Nvidia’s next-generation AI accelerator, Feynman, is expected to arrive,” a third industry source said.
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: Nikkei: TSMC plans to raise prices for both advanced- and mature-node foundry services by up to 10% in 2027, reflecting higher costs for materials, semiconductor manufacturing equipment, and the construction of new overseas fabs. Base price increases will range from 5% to 10%, depending on the customer and product. For additional high-performance computing (HPC) chip orders exceeding customers’ original forecasts, TSMC plans to charge an additional premium of 10% to 15% on top of the base increase. Price negotiations began around June and concluded in July, with the new pricing set to take effect in early 2027.
items.content:
Nikkei: TSMC plans to raise prices for both advanced- and mature-node foundry services by up to 10% in 2027, reflecting higher costs for materials, semiconductor manufacturing equipment, and the construction of new overseas fabs.

Base price increases will range from 5% to 10%, depending on the customer and product.

For additional high-performance computing (HPC) chip orders exceeding customers’ original forecasts, TSMC plans to charge an additional premium of 10% to 15% on top of the base increase.

Price negotiations began around June and concluded in July, with the new pricing set to take effect in early 2027.
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: Hmm… TrendForce expects the NAND supply shortage to ease in 2027. “TrendForce noted that while servers already account for more than 40% of total NAND flash bit demand, smartphones and notebooks still represent nearly 40% combined and therefore continue to have a significant impact on overall demand. In terms of inventory, memory manufacturers’ inventory levels remain relatively low. Although module makers’ inventories have risen because of weakness in the consumer market, inventories held by other buyers remain manageable. Taking these factors together, TrendForce expects the NAND flash bit supply-demand gap to remain at negative 4–5% in 2026, indicating a continued shortage. However, the g
items.content:
Hmm… TrendForce expects the NAND supply shortage to ease in 2027.

“TrendForce noted that while servers already account for more than 40% of total NAND flash bit demand, smartphones and notebooks still represent nearly 40% combined and therefore continue to have a significant impact on overall demand.

In terms of inventory, memory manufacturers’ inventory levels remain relatively low. Although module makers’ inventories have risen because of weakness in the consumer market, inventories held by other buyers remain manageable.

Taking these factors together, TrendForce expects the NAND flash bit supply-demand gap to remain at negative 4–5% in 2026, indicating a continued shortage. However, the gap is expected to turn positive in the second half of 2027, easing the pressure from tight supply.”
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: Anthropic ARR Update Anthropic’s latest ARR has reached $79.5 billion, and its absolute month-over-month increase is expected to exceed $10 billion by the end of this month. 1. ARR increased by $10 billion from $69 billion at the end of June in roughly three weeks. 2. Since March this year, the absolute month-over-month increase in ARR has been no less than $10 billion each month. From March through June, the respective increases were +$10 billion, +$11 billion, +$14 billion, and +$15 billion. Source: YipitData
items.content:
Anthropic ARR Update

Anthropic’s latest ARR has reached $79.5 billion, and its absolute month-over-month increase is expected to exceed $10 billion by the end of this month.

1. ARR increased by $10 billion from $69 billion at the end of June in roughly three weeks.

2. Since March this year, the absolute month-over-month increase in ARR has been no less than $10 billion each month. From March through June, the respective increases were +$10 billion, +$11 billion, +$14 billion, and +$15 billion.

Source: YipitData
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: Chinese media report: Xiaomi is increasing shipments of low- and mid-range smartphones, reflecting its internal assessment that the current memory market may be approaching an inflection point. Xiaomi had already lowered its shipment target twice this year because of short-term shortages of upstream memory chips and persistently rising costs. An industry source told Chinese media that downstream companies, including smartphone manufacturers, have begun to show clear resistance to the ongoing rise in memory prices. In particular, OPPO and vivo reportedly rejected Samsung Electronics’ recently proposed third-quarter pricing, even though the proposed increase was smaller than those in the previ
items.content:
Chinese media report:

Xiaomi is increasing shipments of low- and mid-range smartphones, reflecting its internal assessment that the current memory market may be approaching an inflection point.

Xiaomi had already lowered its shipment target twice this year because of short-term shortages of upstream memory chips and persistently rising costs.

An industry source told Chinese media that downstream companies, including smartphone manufacturers, have begun to show clear resistance to the ongoing rise in memory prices.

In particular, OPPO and vivo reportedly rejected Samsung Electronics’ recently proposed third-quarter pricing, even though the proposed increase was smaller than those in the previous two quarters.

The source explained that Xiaomi’s decision to raise its shipment target signals that downstream memory customers are no longer willing to absorb continuously rising memory costs. Once demand-side price tolerance reaches its limit, the momentum behind further price increases could hit an inflection point.

However, reaching the limit of demand-side price tolerance does not necessarily mean that prices will fall immediately. Multiple industry sources noted that although resistance from downstream customers has begun to emerge, the current memory chip shortage is unlikely to ease in the near term.
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: > > Xiaomi Raises Annual Smartphone Shipment Target on Expectations of a Turnaround in the Memory Market, Including Price Declines • Xiaomi raised its 2026 smartphone shipment target from approximately 90 million units to 110 million units, representing an increase of around 16%. Most of the incremental shipments are expected to come from low-end models. • According to sources close to Xiaomi, the upward revision reflects the company’s internal assessment that current memory market conditions may reverse, with prices potentially beginning to decline. • Xiaomi had previously lowered its annual shipment target twice due to short-term memory chip shortages and persistently rising costs.
items.content:
>> Xiaomi Raises Annual Smartphone Shipment Target on Expectations of a Turnaround in the Memory Market, Including Price Declines

• Xiaomi raised its 2026 smartphone shipment target from approximately 90 million units to 110 million units, representing an increase of around 16%. Most of the incremental shipments are expected to come from low-end models.

• According to sources close to Xiaomi, the upward revision reflects the company’s internal assessment that current memory market conditions may reverse, with prices potentially beginning to decline.

• Xiaomi had previously lowered its annual shipment target twice due to short-term memory chip shortages and persistently rising costs.
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: > > TSMC Expects No Impact on Current Operations from Reports of Japan’s Central Glass Halting High-Purity Tungsten Hexafluoride Production • Amid market reports that Japan’s Central Glass, a gas supplier to TSMC, may halt production of high-purity tungsten hexafluoride (WF₆) due to shortages of raw materials sourced from mainland China, TSMC said it does not currently expect any impact on its operations. • TSMC said that, as part of its global procurement strategy, it continues to develop multi-source supply solutions and diversify its global supplier base, providing resilience against potential supply risks.
items.content:
>> TSMC Expects No Impact on Current Operations from Reports of Japan’s Central Glass Halting High-Purity Tungsten Hexafluoride Production

• Amid market reports that Japan’s Central Glass, a gas supplier to TSMC, may halt production of high-purity tungsten hexafluoride (WF₆) due to shortages of raw materials sourced from mainland China, TSMC said it does not currently expect any impact on its operations.

• TSMC said that, as part of its global procurement strategy, it continues to develop multi-source supply solutions and diversify its global supplier base, providing resilience against potential supply risks.
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 21, 2026
